model_findings.description: GET /healthz unconditionally forks four subprocesses on every request (codex --version, gemini --version, claude --version, hermes --version via _probe_cli). There is no caching, no debounce, and no rate-limit. A trivial HTTP flood from any local process can saturate the process table and stall the host. This is a known surface (issue #2029) that the PR reproduces verbatim. Fix: cache probe results with a TTL (e.g. 30 s) so the subprocess fan-out is amortised, and return the cached state on subsequent calls within the window.

POST /v1/chat/completions accepts an unbounded request body. FastAPI / Starlette have no default body-size cap, so a client can submit a multi-megabyte messages array that is fully parsed and held in memory before any backend is invoked. Combined with the fact that the flattened prompt is then passed to a subprocess, an adversarial or buggy client can cause memory exhaustion. Add a Starlette ContentSizeLimitMiddleware (or equivalent) with a sane cap (e.g. 1 MB) and reject oversized payloads with 413.

The --host argument defaults to 127.0.0.1 but accepts any value without validation. Passing --host 0.0.0.0 silently exposes the proxy on all interfaces, contradicting the 'localhost-only' guarantee stated in the docs and surfacing every local agent CLI to the network. This matches the open issue #2030. The argument parser should either reject values that are not loopback addresses (127.0.0.1 / ::1) or require an explicit --allow-remote flag before binding to non-loopback addresses, raising SystemExit with a clear warning otherwise.

_gemini_backend passes the user-controlled prompt as f"--prompt={prompt}" and _hermes_backend passes it as f"--oneshot={prompt}", both as a single argv element. Although subprocess.run with a list avoids shell injection, many CLI parsers (argparse, click, getopt) re-split on = in the value or treat embedded newlines as flag boundaries when the underlying tool accepts multi-value arguments. A prompt containing =, --, or newlines can cause the downstream CLI to misparse its own flags. The safe pattern is to pass the prompt value as a separate positional argument or via stdin (as _codex_backend already does), keeping the flag name and user data in distinct argv slots.

502 and 504 error responses echo the first line of the backend process's stderr verbatim into the OpenAI-shaped JSON body (via _first_error_line). Backend CLIs routinely emit absolute file paths, internal hostnames, API-key prefixes, or stack-trace fragments in their first error line. Since the proxy is localhost-only for now this is lower risk, but any future --allow-remote mode or reverse-proxy setup would expose these details to remote callers. Sanitise the error string to a generic upstream failure message and log the raw stderr at DEBUG level server-side instead.

Both _gemini_backend and _hermes_backend embed the flattened prompt inline in an argv element. Linux ARG_MAX is typically 2 MB; macOS is 1 MB per argument. A long conversation history (or a single large system prompt) can silently exceed this limit, causing execve to fail with E2BIG. The resulting OSError is caught and re-raised as a 502, but the error message will be confusing. _codex_backend correctly passes the prompt via stdin; _gemini_backend and _hermes_backend should do the same, writing the prompt to a temp file or using Popen with stdin=PIPE as codex does.

GET /v1/models returns created: int(time.time()) on every call, so two successive requests return different timestamps for the same model. The OpenAI spec treats the created field as a stable Unix epoch for when the model was made available; clients that cache or diff the models list will see spurious changes on every poll. Replace with a module-level constant (e.g. the epoch of the proxy's own release date) or omit the field rather than fabricating a moving value.

tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile is created with default permissions (0o600 on most POSIX systems) but the delete=False pattern leaves a window between creation and unlink where the file exists with its output content. On macOS the default umask produces 0o644, making the temp file world-readable. Under concurrent load multiple such files accumulate in /tmp until finally{} runs. Use tempfile.TemporaryDirectory or pass mode='w+b' with an explicit os.chmod(0o600) immediately after creation to harden this.

model_findings.description: The `aliased_required` set is computed to suppress missing-required-field violations when an alias is present, but the suppression logic is incomplete. `aliased_required` contains the *canonical* field name (e.g. `'error'`) when the alias maps to a required field — however the subtraction `required_fields - item_fields - aliased_required` does correctly exclude it. The bug is subtler: the alias-field violation is also emitted for that same alias in the forbidden-field loop immediately above, so an item like `{'sentence': '...', 'incorrect': 'x', 'correction': 'y'}` produces both a 'forbidden field incorrect' violation AND a 'missing required field error' violation. The reader of the report then sees a doubled signal: one saying 'use error not incorrect' and a second saying 'error is missing', which is redundant and contradictory. The forbidden-field violation is the informative one; the missing-required one should be suppressed whenever an alias that maps to that required field is already flagged. Fix: when building `aliased_required`, also suppress the corresponding missing-required entry unconditionally — not only from the subtraction but also by pre-filtering from the 'required_fields' set that drives the missing-field loop.

The canonical shape printed in the diagnostic message shows `correction: "вмиваюся"` and `error: "вмиваюся"` with identical values, which is pedagogically nonsensical — an error-correction item where the error and correction are the same word provides no useful information to a learner and would be flagged by content review. This was likely a copy-paste mistake. The example should demonstrate a real correction, e.g. `error: "вмивуся"` / `correction: "вмиваюся"`, to guide writers toward producing valid content rather than accidentally normalising degenerate items.

The `activity_schema` gate is placed at the very top of `run_python_qg` and returns immediately on failure via the early `_python_qg_report` call, skipping every downstream gate including `word_count`, `plan_sections`, `vesum_verified`, etc. This means a module with both activity-schema violations and, say, a word-count shortfall will only surface the schema error in the QG report; the writer correction loop therefore has no visibility into the other failures and may fix the schema issue and re-submit a module that still has word-count or VESUM failures, wasting a correction round. The gate should accumulate failures rather than short-circuit, consistent with how other non-fatal gates are handled. If early exit is intentional for performance, it must be documented and the correction-loop prompt must explicitly instruct the writer that additional gates will be checked after schema passes.

`_ACTIVITY_ITEM_AUTHORING_FIELDS` and the gate logic only cover `error-correction`. All other activity types (fill-in-the-blank, matching, multiple-choice, dialogue, etc.) silently pass through with `allowed_fields is None` and are never validated. The docstring says 'V1 is intentionally narrow' but there is no follow-up issue reference or TODO to expand coverage. Given that the motivating regression was a field-alias problem that could equally occur in other activity types, the gate gives a false sense of completeness. At minimum, add a TODO with an issue number or a comment that explicitly names the activity types currently unguarded.

When an individual item inside `items` is not a `Mapping` (e.g. a plain string or integer), the gate silently skips it via `continue` without recording any violation and without incrementing `checked`. A writer that emits `items: ["wrong string", ...]` will pass the gate with `checked=0` for those entries, masking a structural error. The gate should either flag non-Mapping items as violations or at minimum increment `checked` so the caller can see that some items were not inspected.

In `test_replays_m20_build8_failure_shape`, each item contains only `{'incorrect': '...'}` — the required `sentence` field is absent. The test asserts only that `incorrect → error` aliasing is flagged, but does not assert that a missing `sentence` violation is also reported. As a result the test does not fully characterise the real failure shape from build #8, and a regression that stops detecting the missing `sentence` would not be caught by this test. The test should also assert that a missing-required `sentence` violation is present in the results.

model_findings.description: The test constructs the citation list by hardcoding page numbers rather than reading `plan_references` from the YAML file it already loads into `plan`. This creates a permanent manual-sync obligation: every time the plan changes, both the YAML and this test must be updated in lockstep, and the commit message explicitly encodes this as a lesson-learned. The robust fix is to derive the input list directly from the plan: `[{"source_ref": r} for r in plan.get("plan_references", [])]`. That way the test always reflects the live plan state, and a drift between the YAML and the test becomes impossible by construction. The current pattern survived this PR only because CI caught the mismatch — next time it may not be noticed until after merge.

The test only exercises the passing path (correct citations → gate returns truthy). There is no assertion that a deliberately wrong citation — e.g. the now-retired `Захарійчук Grade 4, с. 162` string — would cause `_citation_gate` to return falsy. Without a negative case the test cannot distinguish a correctly-working gate from a gate that always returns True, and gives no regression coverage for the exact defect this PR fixed (#1901: off-level reference accepted).

The PR description states it bundles three distinct fixes — VESUM reviewer scope leak, textbook-grounding plan reference issue, and metalinguistic-stripping quote cleanup — but the diff supplied for review contains only the test re-pin for the citation fix. The plan YAML change that motivated this test update (`a1/my-morning.yaml` `plan_references` swap) and both other regression fixes are absent. Reviewing an incomplete diff means the VESUM scope leak and text-cleaning edge cases cannot be assessed; if those fixes were committed separately they should each carry their own test evidence per the project's deterministic-over-hallucination policy.
